text: In this video we will be describing the primary principles of adhesiolysis. As you can see the patient was previous twice abandoned laparotomies with a previous burst abdomen. The most important thing in such a situation is anticipation. We need to anticipate that the small bowel typically a few loops of small bowel would undergo adhesion at the region of the rectus sheath. Adhesiolysis here needs to be very very gentle in order to see to it that the loop of small bowel is liberated completely without causing any serosal damage. The technique is very meticulous as well as very gentle with minimal amount of traction. One may choose to use cold knife or may use the laparoscopic shearer or any other device which one is comfortable with. However, one of the most important principle remains to dissect a part of the sheath which is adjacent to the adherent loop of bowel such that It can be liberated away with slightly free margins from the bowel. Another important is to note very very seriously is that quite a lot of times in between the loop of the bowels there could be adhesions. These adhesions need to be separated off very very gently preferably with blunt dissection.

The surgeon uses a cold knife to separate adhesions between loops of the bowel, carefully liberating the bowel from the minimal separation caused by adhesions. The bowel is visibly freed from the adhesions, which are severe due to previous surgeries. Scissors are used for both blunt and sharp dissection to further separate the adherent bowel, focusing on the mesentery level where adhesions typically occur.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic shearer to apply traction while scissors perform cold dissection to gently separate the bowel infiltrated inside the rectus sheath due to a previous burst abdomen. The loops of ileum are carefully liberated to avoid serosal damage, with potential repair using 3-0 silk sutures if needed.

The surgeon uses a cold knife to release adhesions between loops of bowel, creating windows for easier dissection. A 30-degree camera system aids in maneuvering through spaces, while the laparoscopic shearer provides traction. The bowel loops are rotated along the mesentery and adherent to the anterior abdominal wall. The scissors are used for dissection, maintaining a bloodless plane despite fibrotic tissue. A margin of approximately 0.5 cm of the rectus sheath is dissected away from the anterior abdominal wall to preserve serosal integrity.

The assistant adjusts the camera to face the anterior abdominal wall, aiding in the reverse direction adhesiolysis. The laparoscopic shearer is used for blunt dissection to separate the bowel from the anterior abdominal wall. A large adherent section is gently liberated. The bladder becomes visible along the anterior abdominal wall, and care is taken to avoid damage by cutting closer to the bowel while preserving the surrounding fat.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic shearer to dissect a firm adhesion of the loop of ileum from the anterior abdominal wall. The camera is tilted for optimal visualization, and part of the sheath is sacrificed to preserve the serosal aspect of the ileum. The loop of bowel is visible above the sheath as the shearer separates it.

The surgeon performs dissection to separate the right colon, including the cecum and appendix, from adhesions. The appendix is visualized entering the pelvis, adherent to the ovary due to dense endometriosis. The Bissinger bipolar provides gentle traction while the shearer performs sharp dissection. The ileocecal junction is identified, adherent to the ovary. The uterus remains obscured by the bladder, which has lost its peritoneum and is adherent to the bowel.
